era_context: The early 13th century saw the Ayyubid sultanate at its height under al-Malik al-Kamil, the Fourth Crusade's aftermath still shaping Eastern Mediterranean politics, and crusading fervor continuing in Western Europe. In England, King John was facing mounting pressure from rebellious barons over his military failures and heavy taxation. The Mongol Empire under Temüjin was consolidating power on the steppes, approaching the moment of his proclamation as Genghis Khan. The Delhi Sultanate was in transition following the death of Muhammad of Ghor. East Asia remained divided between the Jin Dynasty in the north and the Song Dynasty in the south.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Mesoamerica and North America (no written records)
Southeast Asia (limited documentation for this specific year)

events.title: Magna Carta granted by King John of England
events.description: King John of England, facing pressure from rebellious barons, granted the Magna Carta (Great Charter) at Runnymede on June 15, 1215. The Magna Carta was a written agreement that constrained royal power and established the principle that even the king was subject to the law. The charter addressed specific grievances of the barons, including arbitrary taxation, feudal incidents, and the treatment of the Church. While the Magna Carta was initially a peace settlement between the king and the barons, it would eventually become a foundational document of constitutional government and the rule of law in the English-speaking world. The charter was reissued and modified multiple times over the following decades, and its significance evolved far beyond its original context. The Magna Carta represented a crucial moment in the development of limited government and the protection of individual rights.

sources.name: The Magna Carta (primary text, original charter of 1215)
Roger of Wendover, Flores Historiarum (contemporary chronicle)

events.certainty_note: The granting of the Magna Carta on June 15, 1215, is one of the most well-attested events in medieval history. The original charter survives, and contemporary chronicles confirm the date and basic facts.

events.title: Fourth Lateran Council convened by Pope Innocent III
events.description: Pope Innocent III convened the Fourth Lateran Council in November 1215, one of the most important church councils of the medieval period. The council addressed a wide range of ecclesiastical and doctrinal issues, including the definition of Catholic doctrine, the conduct of clergy, and the organization of the Church. The council also reaffirmed the Church's commitment to crusading and called for a new crusade against the Islamic world (which would become the Fifth Crusade). The Fourth Lateran Council represented the height of papal authority and the Church's influence over Christendom. The council's decrees would shape Catholic practice and doctrine for centuries to come.
key_figures: Church bishops and prelates from across Christendom

sources.name: Roger of Wendover, Flores Historiarum (contemporary chronicle)

events.certainty_note: The Fourth Lateran Council in November 1215 is well-attested in ecclesiastical records and contemporary chronicles. The date and basic facts are secure.

events.title: Al-Malik al-Aziz dies; al-Malik al-Kamil assumes greater authority
events.description: Al-Malik al-Aziz, the Ayyubid sultan, died in 1215, and his son al-Malik al-Kamil assumed greater authority within the sultanate. Al-Kamil had been serving as a military commander and governor under his father, but al-Aziz's death allowed him to consolidate power more fully. The transition of power was relatively smooth, reflecting the strength of the Ayyubid dynasty at this time. Al-Kamil would go on to become one of the greatest Ayyubid sultans, known for his military acumen and diplomatic skills. His assumption of greater authority in 1215 marked the beginning of a period of Ayyubid strength and expansion.

sources.name: Baha al-Din Ibn Shaddad, al-Nawadir al-Sultaniyya (Ayyubid perspective, compiled later)
Ibn al-Athir, al-Kamil fi al-Ta'rikh (13th-century Islamic chronicle)

events.certainty_note: Al-Malik al-Aziz's death in 1215 is attested in Islamic sources, though the exact date is sometimes given as 1214 or 1216. The year 1215 is the most commonly cited date.

events.title: Temüjin consolidates Mongol power; approaches proclamation as Genghis Khan
events.description: Temüjin continued his consolidation of power on the Mongol steppes, bringing various tribal confederations under his authority. By 1215, he had defeated or subordinated most major rivals, including the Tatars, Kereit, and Jamukha's confederation. This consolidation of power positioned Temüjin for his proclamation as Genghis Khan. The exact date of the proclamation is debated by scholars, with some placing it in 1206 and others in 1220 or later. By 1215, Temüjin was the undisputed leader of the Mongol tribes and was preparing for his expansion into Central Asia. The consolidation of Mongol power under Temüjin represented a major shift in the balance of power on the Eurasian steppes.

sources.name: The Secret History of the Mongols (oral tradition compiled 13th century)
Rashid al-Din, Jami' al-tawarikh (early 14th century compilation)

events.certainty_note: The exact chronology of Temüjin's consolidation is uncertain. The Secret History provides narrative details but lacks precise dating. Scholars debate whether the proclamation as Genghis Khan occurred in 1206 or later (c. 1220). The year 1215 is used here to represent the period of consolidation, but the actual sequence of events may differ.

events.title: Iltutmish emerges as military leader in Delhi Sultanate
events.description: Iltutmish, a former slave-soldier (mamluk), emerged as a prominent military leader in the Delhi Sultanate. By 1215, he had gained the confidence of the sultanate's rulers and was commanding significant military forces. Iltutmish's rise reflected the military-based nature of the early Delhi Sultanate, where power flowed to the most capable military commander rather than following strict hereditary succession. His emergence as a military leader in 1215 marked the beginning of his rise to power, which would eventually lead to his consolidation of the sultanate in the 1220s and 1230s.
events.certainty_note: Iltutmish's emergence as a military leader is attested in Indian sources, but the specific chronology of his advancement is uncertain. The general period of his rise (c. 1215–1236) is secure, but precise dating of individual steps is unclear.

events.title: Jin Dynasty faces military pressure from Song Dynasty
events.description: The Jin Dynasty, which ruled northern China, faced mounting military pressure from the Song Dynasty to the south. The Jin had been in gradual decline since the early 12th century, losing territory and facing succession disputes. By 1215, the Jin Dynasty was experiencing administrative fragmentation and military weakness. The Song Dynasty, under the leadership of capable commanders, was expanding northward and threatening Jin territory. The Jin-Song conflict would continue for decades, with the Jin eventually falling to the Mongols in 1234. The military pressure from the Song in 1215 was one of several challenges facing the Jin Dynasty.

sources.name: Jurchen historical records (compiled in later Chinese histories)
Song Dynasty chronicles (contemporary perspective on Jin affairs)

events.certainty_note: The Jin Dynasty's military pressure from the Song Dynasty in the early 13th century is well-documented in Chinese sources. The specific conditions in 1215 are less precisely dated, but the general trajectory of Jin decline is secure.
cross_references: 1234-001 (Fall of the Jin Dynasty to the Mongols)

disconfirming_evidence: The Magna Carta, while confirmed as granted in 1215, is often misunderstood in modern interpretations. The charter was not a revolutionary document establishing democracy or universal rights; it was a feudal agreement addressing specific baronial grievances and was intended to restore traditional feudal relationships. King John repudiated it almost immediately in 1216, and it was reissued multiple times with significant modifications. The Fourth Lateran Council's call for a crusade (event 1215-002) was not immediately acted upon; the Fifth Crusade was not formally proclaimed until 1217. The exact date of al-Malik al-Aziz's death is sometimes given as 1214 or 1216 rather than 1215. The chronology of Temüjin's consolidation and his proclamation as Genghis Khan is highly disputed; some scholars place the proclamation in 1206, others in 1220 or later.
historiographic_note: The year 1215 is exceptionally well-documented in English sources due to the Magna Carta, which is one of the most famous documents in history. English chronicles provide detailed accounts of the baronial revolt and the granting of the charter. Islamic sources for the Ayyubid sultanate are often compiled decades after events but provide good coverage of al-Kamil's rise. The Fourth Lateran Council is well-attested in ecclesiastical records. Mongol history relies on the Secret History, an oral tradition with inherent dating uncertainties. Indian sources for the Delhi Sultanate are sparse and often contradictory. Chinese sources for the Jin Dynasty are more abundant but focus on court politics. The surviving sources are heavily weighted toward military and political events; economic, social, and cultural developments are underrepresented. Sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas, and Oceania are absent from the historical record for this year.

graph_edges.note: The granting of the Magna Carta in 1215 led to King John's repudiation and the First Barons' War in 1216
The Fourth Lateran Council's call for a crusade in 1215 led to the formal proclamation of the Fifth Crusade in 1217
Al-Malik al-Aziz's death in 1215 led to al-Kamil's consolidation of full control in 1216
Temüjin's consolidation of power in 1215 positioned him for the invasion of Khwarazmia in 1219
Iltutmish's emergence as a military leader in 1215 led to his rise to prominence in 1217
The Jin Dynasty's military pressure from the Song Dynasty in 1215 contributed to its eventual fall to the Mongols in 1234
